BECAUSE no People can be truly happy, though under the greatest Enjoyment of Civil Liberties, if abridged of the Freedom of their Consciences, as to their Religious Profession and Worship: And Almighty God being the only Lord of Conscience, Father of Lights and Spirits; and the Author as well as Object of all divine Knowledge, Faith and Worship, who only doth enlighten the Minds, and persuade and convince the Understanding of People, I do hereby grant and declare, That no Person or Persons, inhabiting in this Province or Territories, who shall confess and acknowledge One almighty God, the Creator, Upholder and Ruler of the World; and profess him or themselves obliged to live quietly under the Civil Government, shall be in any Case molested or prejudiced, in his or their Person or Estate, because of his or their conscientious Persuasion or Practice, nor be compelled to frequent or maintain any religious Worship, Place or Ministry, contrary to his or their Mind.… —William Penn, Charter of Privileges Granted by William Penn, esq. to the Inhabitants of Pennsylvania and Territories, October 28, 1701 Because Penn’s guarantee of religious freedom welcomed many faiths, Pennsylvania became otherwise known as what kind of colony in British America?

This prompt refers to the excerpt below. "Since the foundations of the American commonwealth were laid in colonial times over 300 years ago, vigorous complaint and more or less bitter persecution have been aimed at newcomers to our shores. Also the congressional reports of about 1840 are full of abuse of English, Scotch, Welsh immigrants as paupers, criminals, and so forth. Old citizens in Detroit of Irish and German descent have told me of the fierce tirades and propaganda directed against the great waves of Irish and Germans who came over from 1840 on for a few decades to escape civil, racial, and religious persecution in their native lands. The 'Know-Nothings,' lineal ancestors of the Ku-Klux Klan, bitterly denounced the Irish and Germans as mongrels, scum, foreigners, and a menace to our institutions, much as other great branches of the Caucasian race of glorious history and antecedents are berated to-day….But to-day it is the Italians, Spanish, Poles, Jews, Greeks, Russians, Balkanians, and so forth, who are the racial lepers…..In this bill we find racial discrimination at its worst—a deliberate attempt to go back 84 years in our census taken every 10 years so that a blow may be aimed at peoples of eastern and southern Europe, particularly at our recent allies in the Great War—Poland and Italy." Robert H. Clancy, Congressional Record, 68th Congress, 1st Session, 1924 Which of the following legislative measures best illustrates nativist bias?

This question refers to the following information.
Now, we have organized a society, and we call it "Share Our Wealth Society," a society with the motto "Every Man a King."…
We propose to limit the wealth of big men in the country. There is an average of $15,000 in wealth to every family in America. That is right here today.
We do not propose to divide it up equally. We do not propose a division of wealth, but we do propose to limit poverty that we will allow to be inflicted on any man's family. We will not say we are going to try to guarantee any equality … but we do say that one third of the average is low enough for any one family to hold, that there should be a guarantee of a family wealth of around $5,000; enough for a home, an automobile, a radio, and the ordinary conveniences, and the opportunity to educate their children.…
We will have to limit fortunes. Our present plan is that we will allow no man to own more than $50,000,000. We think that with that limit we will be able to carry out the balance of the program.
—Senator Huey P. Long of Louisiana, Radio Address, February 23, 1934

This question refers to the following information.
At a meeting of working girls held at Hull House during a strike in a large shoe factory, the discussions made it clear that the strikers who had been most easily frightened, and therefore the first to capitulate, were naturally those girls who were paying board and were afraid of being put out if they fell too far behind. After a recital of a case of peculiar hardship one of them exclaimed: "Wouldn't it be fine if we had a boarding club of our own, and then we could stand behind each other in a time like this?" After that events moved quickly. We … discussed all the difficulties and fascinations of such an undertaking, and on the first of May, 1891, two comfortable apartments near Hull House were rented and furnished. The Settlement was responsible for the furniture and paid the first month's rent, but beyond that the members managed the club themselves.… At the end of the third year the club occupied all of the six apartments which the original building contained, and numbered fifty members.
—Jane Addams, Twenty Years at Hull House, 1912

turned out: This question refers to the following passage. At a meeting of working girls held at Hull House during a strike in a large shoe factory, the discussions made it clear that the strikers who had been most easily frightened, and therefore the first to capitulate, were naturally those girls who were paying board and were afraid of being put out if they fell too far behind. After a recital of a case of peculiar hardship one of them exclaimed: "Wouldn't it be fine if we had a boarding club of our own, and then we could stand behind each other in a time like this?" After that events turned out quickly. We … discussed all the difficulties and fascinations of such an undertaking, and on the first of May, 1891, two comfortable apartments near Hull House were rented and furnished. The Settlement was responsible for the furniture and paid the first month's rent, but beyond that the members managed the club themselves.… At the end of the third year the club occupied all of the six apartments which the original building contained, and numbered fifty members. —Jane Addams, Twenty Years at Hull House, 1912 In light of this excerpt, which motivation most likely inspired a settlement house worker to establish mutual aid efforts like a boarding club?

At a meeting of working girls held at Hull House during a strike in a large shoe factory, the discussions made it clear that the strikers who had been most easily frightened, and therefore the first to capitulate, were naturally those girls who were paying board and were afraid of being put out if they fell too far behind. After that events moved quickly. We … discussed all the difficulties and fascinations of such an undertaking, and on the first of May, 1891, two comfortable apartments near Hull House were rented and furnished. The Settlement was responsible for the furniture and paid the first month's rent, but beyond that the members managed the club themselves.… At the end of the third year the club occupied all of the six apartments which the original building contained, and numbered fifty members.
—Jane Addams, Twenty Years at Hull House, 1912

One woman, after hearing an especially harsh story, cried out, “Wouldn't it be fine if we had a boarding club of our own, and then we could stand behind each other in a time like this?” Soon the group explored the challenges and appeals of such a plan, and on May 1, 1891, rented and furnished two comfortable apartments near Hull House, with the Settlement supplying furniture and the first month’s rent while members ran the club themselves. Within three years the club filled all six apartments of the building and had fifty members. This example of a settlement house like Hull House best illustrates reformers’ use of local institutions as a metonymic means to accomplish what broader goal in American cities?

This question refers to the following information.
At a meeting of working girls held at Hull House during a strike in a large shoe factory, the discussions made it clear that the strikers who had been most easily frightened, and therefore the first to capitulate, were naturally those girls who were paying board and were afraid of being put out if they fell too far behind. After a recital of a case of peculiar hardship one of them exclaimed: "Wouldn't it be fine if we had a boarding club of our own, and then we could stand behind each other in a time like this?" After that events moved quickly. We … discussed all the difficulties and fascinations of such an undertaking, and on the first of May, 1891, two comfortable apartments near Hull House were rented and furnished. The Settlement was responsible for the furniture and paid the first month's rent, but beyond that the members managed the club themselves.… At the end of the third year the club occupied all of the six apartments which the original building contained, and numbered fifty members.
—Jane Addams, Twenty Years at Hull House, 1912

This question refers to the following information.
"An act for the more effectual protection of the property of married women:
"§1. The real property of any female who may hereafter marry, and which she shall own at the time of marriage, and the rents, issues, and profits thereof, shall not be subject to the sole disposal of her husband, nor be liable for his debts, and shall continue her sole and separate property, as if she were a single female.
"§2. The real and personal property, and the rents, issues, and profits thereof, of any female now married, shall not be subject to the disposal of her husband; but shall be her sole and separate property, as if she were a single female, except so far as the same may be liable for the debts of her husband heretofore contracted.
"§3. Any married female may take by inheritance, or by gift, grant, devise, or bequest, from any person other than her husband, and hold to her sole and separate use, and convey and devise real and personal property, and any interest or estate therein, and the rents, issues, and profits thereof, in the same manner and with like effect as if she were unmarried, and the same shall not be subject to the disposal of her husband nor be liable for his debts."
—Married Women's Property Act, New York State (1848)

This question refers to the following excerpt. 'An act for the more effectual protection of the property of married women: §1. The real property of any female who may hereafter marry, and which she shall own at the time of marriage, and the rents, issues, and profits thereof, shall not be subject to the sole disposal of her husband, nor be liable for his debts, and shall continue her sole and separate property, as if she were a single female. §2. The real and personal property, and the rents, issues, and profits thereof, of any female now married, shall not be subject to the disposal of her husband; but shall be her sole and separate property, as if she were a single female, except so far as the same may be liable for the debts of her husband heretofore contracted. §3. Any married female may take by inheritance, or by gift, grant, devise, or bequest, from any person other than her husband, and hold to her sole and separate use, and convey and devise real and personal property, and any interest or estate therein, and the rents, issues, and profits thereof, in the same manner and with like effect as if she were unmarried, and the same shall not be subject to the disposal of her husband nor be liable for his debts.' —Married Women's Property Act, New York State (1848) Which continuity in U.S. history is most directly reflected by the ideas in this passage, particularly considering the unequal legal treatment of women under earlier common law?

This prompt cites the following excerpt from the 1912 Progressive Party Platform: "The conscience of the people, in a time of grave national problems, has called into being a new party, born of the nation's sense of justice. We of the Progressive party here dedicate ourselves to the fulfillment of the duty laid upon us by our fathers to maintain the government of the people, by the people and for the people whose foundations they laid. We hold with Thomas Jefferson and Abraham Lincoln that the people are the masters of their Constitution, to fulfill its purposes and to safeguard it from those who, by perversion of its intent, would convert it into an instrument of injustice. In accordance with the needs of each generation the people must use their sovereign powers to establish and maintain equal opportunity and industrial justice, to secure which this Government was founded and without which no republic can endure. "This country belongs to the people who inhabit it. Its resources, its business, its institutions and its laws should be utilized, maintained or altered in whatever manner will best promote the general interest. It is time to set the public welfare in the first place." Given this emphasis on amending institutions and laws to protect the public welfare and ensure equal opportunity and industrial justice, which phrase best captures the core elements of Theodore Roosevelt’s “Square Deal”?

abridged:
Read the excerpt below and identify the main idea it illustrates.

"The Opposition tells us that we ought not to govern a people without their consent. I answer, the rule of liberty that all just government derives its authority from the consent of the governed, applies only to those who are capable of self-government. We govern the Indians without their consent, we govern our territories without their consent, we govern our children without their consent. How do they know that our government would be without their consent? Would not the people of the Philippines prefer the just, human, civilizing government of this Republic to the savage, bloody rule of pillage and extortion from which we have rescued them? And, regardless of this formula of words made only for enlightened, self-governing people, do we owe no duty to the world? Shall we turn these peoples back to the reeking hands from which we have taken them? Shall we abandon them, with Germany, England, Japan, hungering for them? Shall we save them from those nations, to give them a self-rule of tragedy?…Then, like men and not like children, let us on to our tasks, our mission, and our destiny." — Albert J. Beveridge, "The March of the Flag," 1898

Which single idea does this passage most clearly endorse?

This passage is to be used for reference. "The Opposition says we should not rule a people without their consent. I respond that the principle of liberty—that legitimate government is founded on the consent of the governed—applies only to those able to govern themselves. We rule Native peoples without their consent, we rule our territories without their consent, we rule our children without their consent. How can they be sure our government would be rejected by them? Would not the inhabitants of the Philippines choose the just, humane, civilizing rule of this Republic over the savage, bloody regime of plunder and extortion from which we have delivered them? And beyond this phrase reserved for enlightened, self-governing peoples, do we owe no responsibility to the world? Shall we consign these peoples back into the filthy hands we have rescued them from? Shall we abandon them while Germany, England, Japan covet them? Shall we protect them from those powers, only to hand them a tragic experiment in self-rule?…Then, as adults rather than children, let us proceed with our work, our mission, and our destiny." Albert J. Beveridge, "The March of the Flag," 1898. The attitude Beveridge expresses in this excerpt is most representative of which historical concept?

This prompt relates to the excerpt below, amended for clarity: ‘‘The Opposition insists we should not rule peoples without their consent. I reply that the liberty principle — that legitimate government springs from the consent of the governed — applies only to those capable of self-government. We govern the Indians without their consent, we govern our territories without their consent, we govern our children without their consent. How can they know our government would be worse than their current rulers? Would not the people of the Philippines welcome the just, humane, civilizing administration of this Republic rather than the savage, bloody regime of pillage and extortion from which we have rescued them? And apart from this verbal formula meant only for enlightened, self-governing peoples, do we owe no obligations to the world? Shall we return these peoples to the foul hands from which we have taken them? Shall we abandon them while Germany, England, and Japan covet them? Shall we save them from those powers only to hand them a tragic self-rule?…Then, as responsible adults rather than children, let us proceed with our tasks, our mission, and our destiny.’ Based on this passage, which single idea does Beveridge’s rhetoric most clearly exemplify?

contrary: This passage states: "The Opposition tells us that we ought not to govern a people without their consent. I answer, the rule of liberty that all just government derives its authority from the consent of the governed, applies only to those who are capable of self-government. We govern the Indians without their consent, we govern our territories without their consent, we govern our children without their consent. How do they know that our government would be without their consent? Would not the people of the Philippines prefer the just, human, civilizing government of this Republic to the savage, bloody rule of pillage and extortion from which we have rescued them? And, regardless of this formula of words made only for enlightened, self-governing people, do we owe no duty to the world? Shall we turn these peoples back to the reeking hands from which we have taken them? Shall we abandon them, with Germany, England, Japan, hungering for them? Shall we save them from those nations, to give them a self-rule of tragedy?…Then, like men and not like children, let us on to our tasks, our mission, and our destiny." — Albert J. Beveridge, "The March of the Flag," 1898. The following passage is from Albert J. Beveridge’s 1898 speech, 'The March of the Flag': 'The Opposition tells us that we ought not to govern a people without their consent. I answer, the rule of liberty that all just government derives its authority from the consent of the governed, applies only to those who are capable of self-government. We govern the Indians without their consent, we govern our territories without their consent, we govern our children without their consent. How do they know that our government would be without their consent? Would not the people of the Philippines prefer the just, human, civilizing government of this Republic to the savage, bloody rule of pillage and extortion from which we have rescued them? And, regardless of this formula of words made only for enlightened, self-governing people, do we owe no duty to the world? Shall we turn these peoples back to the reeking hands from which we have taken them? Shall we abandon them, with Germany, England, Japan, hungering for them? Shall we save them from those nations, to give them a self-rule of tragedy?…Then, like men and not like children, let us on to our tasks, our mission, and our destiny.' I reply that the principle of liberty — that legitimate government rests on the consent of the governed — applies only to those capable of self-government. We govern the Indians without their consent, we govern our territories without their consent, we govern our children without their consent. How can they know our government would be rejected by them? Would not the people of the Philippines choose the just, humane, civilizing rule of this Republic over the savage, murderous regime of plunder and extortion from which we have delivered them? And apart from this phrase meant only for enlightened, self-governing peoples, do we not owe responsibilities to the world? Shall we return these peoples to the foul hands from which we took them? Shall we abandon them while Germany, England, Japan covet them? Shall we save them from those nations only to give them a tragic form of self-rule?…Then, with the composure of adults rather than children, let us proceed with our work, our mission, and our destiny." Consider the passage below from Albert J. Beveridge’s 1898 speech 'The March of the Flag': 'The Opposition tells us that we ought not to govern a people without their consent. I answer, the rule of liberty that all just government derives its authority from the consent of the governed, applies only to those who are capable of self-government. We govern the Indians without their consent, we govern our territories without their consent, we govern our children without their consent. How do they know that our government would be without their consent? Would not the people of the Philippines prefer the just, human, civilizing government of this Republic to the savage, bloody rule of pillage and extortion from which we have rescued them? And, regardless of this formula of words made only for enlightened, self-governing people, do we owe no duty to the world? Shall we turn these peoples back to the reeking hands from which we have taken them? Shall we abandon them, with Germany, England, Japan, hungering for them? Shall we save them from those nations, to give them a self-rule of tragedy?…Then, like men and not like children, let us on to our tasks, our mission, and our destiny.' Which term best captures the improvement-minded foreign policy attitude exemplified by Beveridge’s argument?

approachable: Read the passage below from Albert J. Beveridge’s 1898 speech, then identify which idea it most clearly embodies: "The Opposition contends we should not rule people without their consent. I reply that the principle of liberty—that legitimate government springs from the consent of the governed—applies only to those fit for self-rule. We govern Native Americans, our territories, and our children without their consent. How can they know our government would be unwelcome? Would not the people of the Philippines prefer the just, civilizing rule of this Republic to the savage, rapacious regime from which we have freed them? And beyond the legalistic phrase used for enlightened self-governing peoples, do we bear no obligation to the world? Shall we thrust these peoples back into the sordid hands that held them? Shall we forsake them while Germany, England, and Japan covet them? Shall we rescue them from those powers only to impose a tragic self-rule?…Then, like adults rather than children, let us proceed with our duties, our mission, and our destiny." Which overarching policy or ideology does Beveridge’s argument most directly represent?

This question refers to the following information.
Now, we have organized a society, and we call it "Share Our Wealth Society," a society with the motto "Every Man a King."…
We propose to limit the wealth of big men in the country. There is an average of $15,000 in wealth to every family in America. That is right here today.
We do not propose to divide it up equally. We do not propose a division of wealth, but we do propose to limit poverty that we will allow to be inflicted on any man's family. We will not say we are going to try to guarantee any equality … but we do say that one third of the average is low enough for any one family to hold, that there should be a guarantee of a family wealth of around $5,000; enough for a home, an automobile, a radio, and the ordinary conveniences, and the opportunity to educate their children.…
We will have to limit fortunes. Our present plan is that we will allow no man to own more than $50,000,000. We think that with that limit we will be able to carry out the balance of the program.
—Senator Huey P. Long of Louisiana, Radio Address, February 23, 1934

Now, we have organized a society, and we call it "Share Our Wealth Society," a society with the motto "Every Man a King."…

Now, we have organized a society, and we call it "Share Our Wealth Society," a society with the motto "Every Man a King."…

We will have to limit fortunes. Our present plan is that we will allow no man to own more than $50,000,000. We think that with that limit we will be able to carry out the balance of the program.
—Senator Huey P. Long of Louisiana, Radio Address, February 23, 1934

"Now, we have organized a society, and we call it 'Share Our Wealth Society,' a society with the motto 'Every Man a King.' … We propose to limit the wealth of big men in the country. There is an average of $15,000 in wealth to every family in America. That is right here today. We do not propose to divide it up equally. We do not propose a division of wealth, but we do propose to limit poverty that we will allow to be inflicted on any man's family. We will not say we are going to try to guarantee any equality … but we do say that one third of the average is low enough for any one family to hold, that there should be a guarantee of a family wealth of around $5,000; enough for a home, an automobile, a radio, and the ordinary conveniences, and the opportunity to educate their children. … We will have to limit fortunes. Our present plan is that we will allow no man to own more than $50,000,000. We think that with that limit we will be able to carry out the balance of the program." —Senator Huey P. Long of Louisiana, Radio Address, February 23, 1934?

This question refers to the following information.
I come not to urge personal claims, nor to seek individual benefits; I appear as the advocate of those who cannot plead their own cause; I come as the friend of those who are deserted, oppressed, and desolate. In the Providence of God, I am the voice of the maniac whose piercing cries from the dreary dungeons of your jails penetrate not your Halls of Legislation. I am the Hope of the poor crazed beings who pine in the cells, and stalls, and cages, and waste rooms of your poor-houses. I am the Revelation of hundreds of wailing, suffering creatures, hidden in your private dwellings, and in pens and cabins—shut out, cut off from all healing influences, from all mind-restoring cares.… Could their melancholy histories be spread before you as revealed to my grieved spirit during the last three months, how promptly, how earnestly would you search out the most approved means of relief; how trifling, how insignificant, by comparison, would appear the sacrifices you are asked to make; how would a few dimes and dollars, gathered from each citizen, diminish in value as a possession, compared with the certain benefits and vast good to be secured for the suffering insane...by the consecration and application of a sufficient fund to the construction of a suitable hospital.…
—Dorothea Dix, Memorial Soliciting a State Hospital for the Protection and Cure of the Insane,
Submitted to the General Assembly of North Carolina, November 1848

This prompt refers to the following excerpt. I do not press personal claims or seek private gain; I speak for those who cannot speak for themselves; I stand for the abandoned, oppressed, and desolate. By God's Providence, I speak for the deranged whose agonized cries from the bleak cells of your jails do not reach your legislative halls. I speak for the desperate poor souls who languish in the cells, stalls, cages, and waste rooms of your almshouses. I reveal the suffering of hundreds of wailing, afflicted persons hidden in private homes, pens, and cabins—cut off from all healing influences and restorative care.… If their sorrowful stories could be laid before you as shown to my grieving spirit over the past three months, how promptly and earnestly would you seek the best means of relief; how small and insignificant would seem the sacrifices you are asked to make; how little would a few dimes and dollars taken from each citizen compare, as a possession, with the certain benefits and great good to be secured for the suffering insane by dedicating and applying sufficient funds to build a suitable hospital.… —Dorothea Dix, Memorial Soliciting a State Hospital for the Protection and Cure of the Insane, Submitted to the General Assembly of North Carolina, November 1848 Which choice below most accurately captures Dorothea Dix’s viewpoint in this passage?

This question refers to the following information.
I come not to urge personal claims, nor to seek individual benefits; I appear as the advocate of those who cannot plead their own cause; I come as the friend of those who are deserted, oppressed, and desolate. In the Providence of God, I am the voice of the maniac whose piercing cries from the dreary dungeons of your jails penetrate not your Halls of Legislation. I am the Hope of the poor crazed beings who pine in the cells, and stalls, and cages, and waste rooms of your poor-houses. I am the Revelation of hundreds of wailing, suffering creatures, hidden in your private dwellings, and in pens and cabins—shut out, cut off from all healing influences, from all mind-restoring cares.… Could their melancholy histories be spread before you as revealed to my grieved spirit during the last three months, how promptly, how earnestly would you search out the most approved means of relief; how trifling, how insignificant, by comparison, would appear the sacrifices you are asked to make; how would a few dimes and dollars, gathered from each citizen, diminish in value as a possession, compared with the certain benefits and vast good to be secured for the suffering insane...by the consecration and application of a sufficient fund to the construction of a suitable hospital.…
—Dorothea Dix, Memorial Soliciting a State Hospital for the Protection and Cure of the Insane,
Submitted to the General Assembly of North Carolina, November 1848

This question refers to the following information.
I come not to urge personal claims, nor to seek individual benefits; I appear as the advocate of those who cannot plead their own cause; I come as the friend of those who are deserted, oppressed, and desolate. In the Providence of God, I am the voice of the maniac whose piercing cries from the dreary dungeons of your jails penetrate not your Halls of Legislation. I am the Hope of the poor crazed beings who pine in the cells, and stalls, and cages, and waste rooms of your poor-houses. I am the Revelation of hundreds of wailing, suffering creatures, hidden in your private dwellings, and in pens and cabins—shut out, cut off from all healing influences, from all mind-restoring cares.… Could their melancholy histories be spread before you as revealed to my grieved spirit during the last three months, how promptly, how earnestly would you search out the most approved means of relief; how trifling, how insignificant, by comparison, would appear the sacrifices you are asked to make; how would a few dimes and dollars, gathered from each citizen, diminish in value as a possession, compared with the certain benefits and vast good to be secured for the suffering insane...by the consecration and application of a sufficient fund to the construction of a suitable hospital.…
—Dorothea Dix, Memorial Soliciting a State Hospital for the Protection and Cure of the Insane,
Submitted to the General Assembly of North Carolina, November 1848

I do not speak to promote my own claims or to gain personal advantage; I stand as an advocate for those who cannot speak for themselves; I come as a friend to the abandoned, oppressed, and desolate. By God's providence I voice the cries of the mad whose piercing laments from the gloomy dungeons of your jails do not reach your legislative halls. I represent the hope of the poor, distressed souls who languish in the cells, stalls, cages, and spare rooms of your almshouses. I expose the plight of hundreds of wailing, suffering individuals hidden in private homes, pens, and cabins—cut off from healing influences and mind-restoring care.… If their sorrowful histories were laid before you as revealed to my grieving spirit over the past three months, how quickly and earnestly would you seek the best means of relief; how trivial would the sacrifices you are asked to make seem in comparison; how small would a few dimes and dollars collected from each citizen appear when weighed against the certain benefits and great good to be gained for the suffering insane by dedicating and applying adequate funds to build a proper hospital.… —Dorothea Dix, Memorial Soliciting a State Hospital for the Protection and Cure of the Insane, Submitted to the General Assembly of North Carolina, November 1848 Which choice most accurately captures Dix’s viewpoint in this passage, framed in a debatable way?

turned out: This prompt refers to the following passage. "In one view the slaveholders have a decided advantage over all opposition. It is well to notice this advantage—the advantage of complete organization. They are organized; and yet were not at the pains of creating their organizations. The State governments, where the system of slavery exists, are complete slavery organizations. The church organizations in those States are equally at the service of slavery; while the Federal Government, with its army and navy, from the chief magistracy in Washington, to the Supreme Court, and thence to the chief marshalship at New York, is pledged to support, defend, and propagate the crying curse of human bondage. counterfactual: Counterfactually imagining an alternative late-1800s intellectual climate, how do William Graham Sumner’s remarks about natural selection and the survival of the fittest illustrate the era’s tendency to argue that the wealthy and successful occupied their positions because cultural and intellectual beliefs portrayed their rise as appropriate and inevitable?

If, counterfactually, historians treated William Graham Sumner’s argument as emblematic of Gilded Age thought, how would his social-Darwinist rationale—that the wealthy are naturally selected and their dominance is proper and unavoidable—best explain prevailing late-19th-century cultural and intellectual attitudes?

If one imagines an alternate Gilded Age in which social Darwinist ideas like William Graham Sumner's prevailed widely, which interpretation of late-19th-century U.S. society would best fit his claim that the wealthy are the natural products of selection and their status is proper and unavoidable?
metonymic: Refer to the excerpt: Sumner argues that social hierarchies and wealth accumulation are the natural outcomes of selection, likening millionaires to statesmen and scientists as products of an impersonal process; this passage most closely exemplifies which late-19th-century tendency to use scientific or cultural metaphors to portray the success of elites as fitting and unavoidable?

Read this passage from William Graham Sumner (1883): “A drunkard in the gutter is just where he ought to be... The law of survival of the fittest was not made by man, and it cannot be abrogated by man... The millionaires are a product of natural selection... It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands.” How does Sumner’s use of social-scientific language and metonymic reference to “millionaires” reflect late-19th-century American attitudes about social hierarchy and economic success?

How does William Graham Sumner’s metaphor of natural selection—portraying millionaires and statesmen as those ‘selected’ to concentrate wealth while the ‘drunkard in the gutter’ is left behind—serve as a metonymic expression of late 19th-century beliefs that justified the socioeconomic dominance of elites?
reorganized: How does William Graham Sumner’s passage, which argues that natural selection explains and legitimizes the concentration of wealth and the social position of millionaires while casting the poor as appropriately disposed, illustrate reorganized late-19th-century sociological thought about why socioeconomic hierarchies were considered natural and justified?

This question refers to the following information.
"A drunkard in the gutter is just where he ought to be. . . . The law of survival of the fittest was not made by man, and it cannot be abrogated by man. We can only, by interfering with it, produce the survival of the unfittest. . . . The millionaires are a product of natural selection, acting on the whole body of men to pick out those who can meet the requirement of certain work to be done. In this respect they are just like the great statesmen, or scientific men, or military men. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. Let one of them make a mistake and see how quickly the concentration gives way to dispersion."
—William Graham Sumner, What Social Classes Owe to Each Other, 1883

"A drunkard in the gutter is just where he ought to be. . . . The law of survival of the fittest was not made by man, and it cannot be abrogated by man. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. —William Graham Sumner, What Social Classes Owe to Each Other, 1883

The millionaires are a product of natural selection... it is because they are thus selected that wealth... aggregates under their hands." Which idea about late-19th-century American thought does this passage best illustrate?
reverse:
This question refers to the following information.
"A drunkard in the gutter is just where he ought to be. . . . The law of survival of the fittest was not made by man, and it cannot be abrogated by man. We can only, by interfering with it, produce the survival of the unfittest. . . . The millionaires are a product of natural selection, acting on the whole body of men to pick out those who can meet the requirement of certain work to be done. In this respect they are just like the great statesmen, or scientific men, or military men. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. Let one of them make a mistake and see how quickly the concentration gives way to dispersion."
—William Graham Sumner, What Social Classes Owe to Each Other, 1883
The sociological ideas of William Graham Sumner reflect the idea that during the late 1800s

undeserving:
This question refers to the following information.
"A drunkard in the gutter is just where he ought to be. . . . The law of survival of the fittest was not made by man, and it cannot be abrogated by man. We can only, by interfering with it, produce the survival of the unfittest. . . . The millionaires are a product of natural selection, acting on the whole body of men to pick out those who can meet the requirement of certain work to be done. In this respect they are just like the great statesmen, or scientific men, or military men. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. Let one of them make a mistake and see how quickly the concentration gives way to dispersion."
—William Graham Sumner, What Social Classes Owe to Each Other, 1883
